text: The robotic-assisted repair of a right indirect inguinal hernia is underway. The hernia is visible lateral to the epigastric vessels. The surgeon enters above the transversalis fascia, dealing with thin peritoneum, and extends the flap from lateral to medial. The bladder is dropped to expose the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament, crossing the midline to the patient's left side.

The surgeon works laterally to re-enter the preperitoneal plane, identifying and sweeping down the chord structures from the indirect hernia sac. Minor bleeding occurs during the procedure, visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon continues to carefully dissect the cord structures from the indirect hernia sac, progressing down into the inguinal canal. The vas deferens is visible, cleared from the sac, allowing safe continuation without risk of transecting the cord structures. As the dissection proceeds, minor bleeding is observed laterally along the cord and in Bogros' space, causing the area to appear red but not enough to halt the procedure.

The surgeon inspects the surgical field for sources of bleeding while continuing to parietalize the cord structures. Despite the presence of blood in the field, no lipoma of the cord is identified. The surgeon prepares to introduce a sponge to manage the bleeding.

The surgeon uses gravity to unroll a sponge from top to bottom, identifying bleeding areas and applying short bursts of monopolar, quadrary, or bipolar energy to cauterize them. The surgeon carefully lifts and elevates structures to prevent thermal spread, particularly near the Cremaster fibers underneath the cord. After ensuring no small bowel or ureter is nearby, the surgeon checks for a lipoma of the cord, confirming its absence.
The surgeon places a 12 by 16 mesh over the hernia defect, ensuring it is properly positioned within the surgical field.
The surgeon closes the flap using a six-inch relock technique, addressing an additional rent in the flap with a 3-0 silk suture to prevent pooling of blood and potential hematoma formation.

The surgeon places an angiocath into the flap and evacuates gas laparoscopically, pressing on the scrotum to ensure complete air removal. The angiocath is connected to suction, and the upper lip of the mesh is adjusted to sit flat, ensuring the mesh remains flat throughout the procedure as desufflation occurs.
